rescue_reasoning: Keep because the climate_screen indicates explicit_parent: the policy statement sits under “Contribution to Adaptation N°2 (A2).” The measure targets rural drinking water service quality via oversight: “95% of Rural Health Systems will be inspected and registered, ensuring the quality standards of rural drinking water.” This is consistent with adaptation (maintaining safe water services/quality as a resilience outcome) even if no explicit hazard token is present in the text.
policy_statement: By 2030, inspect and register 95% of Rural Health Systems to ensure rural drinking water quality standards (A7d).
mechanism_description: The policy inspects and registers rural health systems to ensure rural drinking water meets quality standards.
primary_causal_pathway: Water system governance/operations → ↑ monitoring + ↑ compliance → ↓ contamination/service failure during climate stresses → ↓ exposure/health risk
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes: Rural drinking-water providers/systems are inspected, registered, and brought under routine oversight with defined quality set. (2) Mediating process: Enhanced monitoring, reporting, and set improves treatment performance and early detection/correction of contaminants, including those that spike during extreme rainfall, flooding, drought, or source-water degradation. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced exposure and sensitivity of rural populations to climate-driven water-quality hazards and supply unreliability (public-health risk reduction and greater system robustness).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced vulnerability to climate-amplified drinking-water contamination and service disruptions through improved monitoring and compliance.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: governance/inspection → improved water-quality assurance → reduced exposure to climate-exacerbated contamination and failures (hazard-to-risk reduction).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the mechanism does not primarily target GHG mitigation or “same service with less energy/material”; it targets safe potable water under varying conditions, aligning best with adaptation (reducing vulnerability).  
3) Edge cases applied: if inspections drove major leak reduction or energy optimization, it could lean Resource Efficiency; if it mandated low-carbon treatment energy, it could support Mitigation—but those are not inherent to this mechanism.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy whose core mechanism is inspection/registration for drinking-water quality assurance principally strengthens resilience and reduces risk from water-quality hazards, including climate-amplified ones.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., health adaptation, water-supply resilience) is deferred to policy-level evidence and wording.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the inspection/registration program is explicitly framed solely as routine public-health regulation with no climate-hazard linkage, the climate classification could be debated; however, for climate-policy taxonomy, improved drinking-water system robustness is typically treated as Adaptation due to reduced vulnerability to extreme events and source-water changes.
key_indicators: “By 2030”; “95%”; “will be inspected and registered”; “ensuring the quality standards of rural drinking water”; “quality standards”
co_benefits: General public health improvement (reduced waterborne disease burden) beyond climate-related contamination spikes.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although phrased as a 2030 percentage goal (target-like), the core action is mandatory inspection/registration tied to enforcing water-quality standards, so it fits best as a regulatory mandate rather than target-setting.
